corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

The last column shows the average minimum distortion that was necessary to reach 0% accuracy on the whole training set.
Deep neural networks are highly expressive models that have recently achieved state of the art performance on speech and visual recognition tasks.

In addition, the specific nature of these perturbations is not a random artifact of learning: the same perturbation can cause a different network, that was trained on a different subset of the dataset, to misclassify the same input.

In addition, the specific nature of these perturbations is not a random artifact of learning: the same perturbation can cause a different network, that was trained on a different subset of the dataset, to misclassify the same input.
But as the resulting computation is automatically discovered by backpropagation via supervised learning, it can be difficult to interpret and can have counter-intuitive properties.
Consider a state-of-the-art deep neural network that generalizes well on an object recognition task.
We expect such network to be robust to small perturbations of its input, because small perturbation cannot change the object category of an image.
Yet, we found that adversarial examples are relatively robust, and are shared by neural networks with varied number of layers, activations or trained on different subsets of the training data.

That is, if we use one neural net to generate a set of adversarial examples, we find that these examples are still statistically hard for another neural network even when it was trained with different hyperparameters or, most surprisingly, when it was trained on a different set of examples.

We perform a number of experiments on a few different networks and three datasets : • For the MNIST dataset, we used the following architectures -A simple fully connected network with one or more hidden layers and a Softmax classifier.
We refer to this network as "AE". • The ImageNet dataset . -Krizhevsky et. al architecture .
Moreover, in some experiments we split the MNIST training dataset into two disjoint datasets P 1 , and P 2 , each with 30000 training cases.
A binary car classifier was trained on top of the last layer features without fine-tuning.
In our future work, we plan to compare these effects in a systematic manner.
For space considerations, we just present results for a representative subset (see Table ) of the MNIST experiments we performed.

Does the hardness of the generated examples rely solely on the particular choice of our training set as a sample or does this effect generalize even to models trained on completely different training sets?
